data.title: Physically-Backed Gold ETFs Do Not Lend Their Gold
data.description:
Let me be crystal clear and upfront — gold ETFs do not lend their gold.

This includes SPDR Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:GLD), iShares Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:IAU), SPDR Gold Mini Shares Trust (NYSEARCA:GLDM)1 and all the additional US listed gold ETFs.2

Simply put, it is not permitted by the product design, nor is it permitted under the regulatory approval granted to each product. Yet, I can’t tell you how many times I’ve cringed hearing a market professional mischaracterize the market by saying that they borrow gold from gold ETFs or that a gold ETF lends its gold. Nothing could be further from the truth.

Why US-listed Physically-backed Gold ETFs Cannot Lend Gold

Gold ETFs hold only one asset: gold. There are three key distinctions that provide assurance the underlying assets are never lent:

  • No regulatory approval has been provided to lend any of the underlying assets held by the fund/trust.
  • No economic benefits from activities related to securities lending are received by the fund/trust.
  • No risk factors are cited related to securities lending, as it is not a feature embedded in the structure.

Additionally, if an ETF engages in securities lending, there are required disclosures informing investors that the fund may lend the underlying assets and of the associated risks.3 By contrast, US-listed physically-backed gold ETFs have no such disclosures, and the publicly filed agreements governing the trusts do not allow for securities lending by the trusts.4

What Happens Instead: Borrowing and Lending ETF Shares

Now that we are clear that US-listed physically-backed gold ETFs do not lend any gold, let's discuss what is possible once a gold ETF becomes established, liquid and mainstream.

As a gold ETF becomes well-adopted into the market, it often becomes a key component of the capital markets ecosystem around the asset it represents. This is the case with GLD and IAU, for example, which have significant levels of AUM, high volumes on the US equity exchanges, and are regularly available for borrowing and lending in the securities finance markets.

Here’s how it works: Traders and speculators often borrow shares of such a gold ETF through standard securities lending relationships, which involve the gold ETF’s shareholders, and do not involve the London gold lease market, or the trust, itself.

Those looking to access bullion short will use the securities finance market to borrow shares of the gold ETF. These shares are lent by the shareholder, not by the trust. For the borrower to access a short gold position, they need to either short that gold ETF (which carries risk) or redeem the shares for physical gold, which can then be sold in the London market.

Importantly, these transactions are conducted between owners of the gold ETF, the custody bank that maintains a “lending program” for the client who owns the gold ETF and those who are borrowing the shares.

The following are also true:

  • All fees, income and risks associated with that transaction are separate and distinct from the activities of the fund or trust;
  • The transaction has no bearing on the gold ETF’s ability to perform its role as an appropriate access mechanism to a unit trust that provides the price performance of gold with little or no variance from the reference price (in this case, the LBMA Gold Price PM);
  • No economics are received by the gold ETF; and
  • No risk of recall, no risk of market dislocation and no risk of credit default are taken by the gold ETF.

When it is time for this independent lending transaction to close out, the borrower of the gold ETF will either buy shares in the secondary market to cover their borrow or unwind by purchasing gold in the OTC market. Once the gold is purchased, using the proper channels, they will submit that gold to create gold ETF shares and use those new shares to close out the borrow.

Either way, the borrowing and unwinding of the trade will result in a healthy level of activity in both the primary market (OTC London bullion market) and the secondary market (listed exchange volume in the US), which will match buyers and sellers. Any imbalance again gets handled by creation or redemption with approved authorized participants.

Market Benefits of Physically-backed Gold ETFs

This type of activity illustrates how gold ETFs add liquidity to the overall gold market ecosystem. Increased trading volumes and the participation of more market participants lead to tighter bid-offer spreads, reducing costs for investors and improving market efficiency. Additionally, these ETFs make gold a more mainstream investment instrument by eliminating the challenges of trading physical gold, such as storage, transfer, or questions about gold type or authenticity.

Conclusion

To summarize: US-listed physically-backed gold ETFs, including GLD and IAU, do not lend their gold. However, their role in the secondary market indirectly contributes to the overall liquidity and sophistication of the gold market, making gold a more accessible and appealing investment option.

Footnotes

1See the most recent prospectus for iShares Gold Trust available here, SPDR Gold Trust available here, SPDR Gold Mini Shares Trust available here.

2Gold ETF: Stock, Holdings and Flows | World Gold Council.

3For example, see the most recent prospectus for iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) available here and SPDR Portfolio Long Term Treasury ETF (SPTL) available here.

4For example, see the most recent prospectus for iShares Gold Trust available here and SPDR Gold Trust available here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Genmab: Positive 2025 Outlook, Johnson & Johnson's Decision Coming Soon
data.description:
Genmab (GMAB) reported strong Q4 2024 results last week and provided 2025 revenue guidance that was slightly ahead of the Street consensus and expense guidance that was below expectations. The stock has rebounded since the earnings report, but it was under pressure since my previous update as investors and traders contemplate the fate of GEN3014, the next-generation CD38 HexaBody on which partner Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) will make a decision to opt-in (or out) in the following weeks.

As I wrote previously, I expect J&J to opt out and return the rights to Genmab, and that further development of this candidate by Genmab itself is unlikely as the agreement with J&J prevents the company from competing in indications Darzalex is approved for. I am long Genmab because I am bullish on the rest of the company and believe the market is already on the side of extreme pessimism considering the current valuation and the growth prospects of the company’s product portfolio, royalty portfolio, pipeline, and prospects for further expansion through the use of the growing cash flows for business development.

Strong end to the year with nearly $900 million in quarterly revenue

Genmab reported $897.4 million in Q4 2024 revenue, beating the analyst consensus by $8.7 million and delivering 29% Y/Y growth. Most products closed the year strong with Darzalex royalties leading the way.

Epkinly net sales were $78 million, and this was slightly lower than expected due to one-time adjustments in France that impacted international revenue in the quarter. Genmab and partner AbbVie (ABBV) are seeing continued good traction of Epkinly, and Genmab says that physicians are providing good feedback since last summer’s approval for relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma.

Tivdak also had a good fourth quarter with $39 million in net sales, and Genmab and partner Pfizer (PFE) recently decided to rearrange the collaboration. As of January 1, 2025, Genmab is in charge of Tivdak's commercialization in all countries outside of the U.S. and China. Genmab will pay Pfizer royalties on net sales in ex-U.S. and ex-China territories in the low teens versus a 50:50 profit split in the United States.

The transition of other territories to Genmab should allow continued expansion of Genmab’s commercial infrastructure ahead of potential approvals and launches of Rina-S and acasunlimab later this decade. Tivdak received positive CHMP opinion for the treatment of second-line plus recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer in January, and EMA approval and launch should follow in the second quarter. Approval and launch in Japan is also expected in the following months.

Guidance for 2025 exceeds expectations despite lower collaboration revenue; expense guidance well below expectations

Total revenue guidance for 2025 was slightly ahead of the Street consensus when the company reported the results ($3.50 billion versus $3.48 billion), and it looks good considering the split – strong growth of recurring revenues versus lower collaboration revenue. Total revenue is expected to grow 12% at the mid-point and recurring revenue 18% at the mid-point of the range.

The most surprising part was the expense guidance range of $2.1-2.2 billion, as it was well below the management’s comments on the Q3 earnings call where they said that the $2.4 billion expense consensus for 2025 looks appropriate.

This translates to only 7% expense growth in 2025 at the mid-point of the range, and is a pleasant surprise after years of significant increases in spending as the company expanded its wholly owned pipeline and focused on building the commercial infrastructure.

Genmab is also prioritizing late-stage investments in Epkinly, Rina-S, and acasunlimab, and it decided to discontinue several early-stage programs last year.

I see room for topline outperformance based on the growth trends of Darzalex, Kesimpta, and Rybrevant among the royalty products, and from Epkinly among the co-commercialized products, and expect this year’s revenue growth to be closer to the high end of the $3.34-3.66 billion guidance range, with recurring revenue growth in the low 20s versus the 18% mid-point of the range.

J&J’s decision on GEN3014 expected in 2-3 weeks

The multiyear saga on GEN3014 is approaching its conclusion, or its next stage. Genmab delivered the head-to-head phase 2 results of GEN3014 versus Darzalex to partner J&J in early January and this opened a 60-day window for J&J to make a decision on the asset – to in-license it, or not.

The fact that this decision has not been made yet provides some hope that J&J may take up the option after all, but I would interpret the lack of an update as something positive. It could just be that J&J opted out of the agreement and that the two parties are now renegotiating the deal, as Genmab previously stated they are open to that possibility as they may want to develop GEN3014 on their own. The current deal terms prevent Genmab from competing with J&J and Darzalex, and if it gets back the rights, Genmab could only explore GEN3014 in indications where Darzalex is not approved.

Alternatively, if the results are so good, why is J&J taking its time to make the decision? There is a potential explanation for a positive or a negative outcome.

My view remains the same – that J&J will opt out of the deal, and that Genmab will decide not to further develop this candidate. My reasoning is based on the following:

  • Unimpressive phase 1/2 results of GEN3014 that Genmab presented at ASH in December 2023. In this trial in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma patients, GEN3014 achieved a 55% response rate (6 of 11 patients) with 1 stringent complete response, 2 very good partial responses, and 3 partial responses.

  • Genmab not presenting follow-up data from the phase 1/2 trial.
  • Darzalex itself is a very potent drug that may not have left room for improved efficacy, and this is the main thesis for GEN3014 – significantly improved preclinical potency versus Darzalex.
  • Genmab says that GEN3014 is significantly more potent than Darzalex based on preclinical assays, but the recommended phase 2 dose of 16mg/kg is identical to the approved dose of Darzalex. This is another piece of evidence pointing to lack of translation of preclinical efficacy into the clinic. Significantly increased potency should (at least in theory) translate to a lower required dose of GEN3014 versus Darzalex.
  • J&J is showing interest and pursuing other mechanisms for the treatment of multiple myeloma, such as two other products Genmab out-licensed to J&J – Tecvayli and Talvey (and including their combination which has shown promising preliminary results in relapsed/refractory patients), and also products like CAR-T therapy Carvykti.

And while I do not expect Genmab to pursue GEN3014 on its own, especially if the efficacy and safety look similar to Darzalex, I would leave the door open for a standalone pursuit of its development if the data are positive or at least clearly trending in GEN3014’s direction. Potential areas of interest would be multiple myeloma patients refractory to Darzalex (which means GEN3014 does not compete with Darzalex itself), or potentially autoimmune diseases.

Pipeline updates

Genmab said that late-stage trials of Epkinly are progressing ahead of schedule and that we should see registrational data from three trials before the end of 2026 – second line+ follicular lymphoma (‘FL’), frontline diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (‘DLBCL’), and second line+ DLBCL for transplant ineligible patients. These readouts should translate to 2H 2027 launches and growth acceleration for Epkinly as these addressable markets are considerably larger than current late-line indications in FL and DLBCL.

The other new pipeline update is the advancement of Rina-S into a phase 3 trial in second line+ endometrial cancer patients by the end of the year. We have not yet seen the early-stage data, but management sounded optimistic and said that “it is the most robust efficacy signal that is currently being generated in that new population” and that they are “very excited about that data.”

Rina-S is already in a phase 3 trial in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer ('PROC') patients and the company continues to guide a 2027 launch with expectations for peak sales in excess of $2 billion.

This year’s data updates will be:

  • Updated results from the expansion cohort of the trial of Rina-S in PROC patients.
  • Initial results of Rina-S in patients with second line+ endometrial cancer.
  • Updated results from the phase 2 trial of acasunlimab in second line+ non-small cell lung cancer patients.
  • Topline results from the head-to-head trial of GEN3014 versus Darzalex and the decision of partner J&J on whether to in-license GEN3014.

Financial review – strong balance sheet and cash flows to facilitate additional pipeline expansion deals

Genmab ended 2024 with nearly $3 billion in cash and no debt, and based on the guidance for 2025, we should see the cash flows exceed $1 billion this year.

The company is in its strongest financial position ever and the cash balance and cash flows should lead to additional pipeline-expanding deals in the following quarters and years, and I assume they will be of similar size to last year’s $1.8 billion acquisition of ProfoundBio and of similar profile – mid/late-stage candidates that could start generating revenues before the end of the decade.

The company will also utilize some of the cash to buy back shares this year – approximately $370 million.

Conclusion

Genmab is entering 2025 with a strong top and bottom-line outlook, and in anticipation of J&J’s decision on GEN3014. I continue to see the investment thesis as very robust even with what I expect to be a negative decision for Genmab and believe that a negative outcome has not only been priced in, but that there is extreme pessimism in the stock related to this asset.

Once the decision is made, the company and its investors can finally shift focus to commercial execution and other pipeline growth drivers, because even if J&J opts in, we will not hear much about this program in the next few years, since it will take quite a bit of time to enroll new trials and generate results.

data.title: Maybe Time To Consider International Equities
data.description:
For nearly fifteen years, international equities, as measured by the iShares MSCI All Country World Index ex U.S. (ACWX), have underperformed the U.S. market, or S&P 500 Index, by a substantial margin. The green line on the below chart represents the performance of the S&P 500 Index and the index is up over 426% since late 2011 while the MSCI All Country World Index ex US is up only 122%.

As noted in a recent article (PDF) by Hartford Funds, since 1975, the outperformance cycle for U.S. stocks versus international stocks has occurred over approximately an eight-year cycle. The current cycle has U.S. stocks outperforming international stocks for almost fourteen years, six years longer than the average cycle.

An important variable for investors to consider when investing internationally is the strength or weakness of one's home currency. For a U.S. investor, a strengthening dollar serves as a headwind for international returns as more of the foreign currency is needed to convert back to a Dollar, thus reducing the US Dollar equivalent return. The green line in the below chart from Brandes Investment Partners represents the U.S. Dollar. A rising line signifies a strengthening Dollar. The dark or green circles on the chart represent the low point for the Dollar before it resumes its move higher or strengthens. The green circles coincide with the green shading for the return columns, comparing the return of the MSCI USA Index versus the MSCI EAFE Index (Developed International.) The grey circles highlight the peak in the Dollar before the line trends lower or the Dollar weakens. The grey circles coincide with the grey column headings and in a weakening Dollar environment, international returns benefit from the weakening Dollar.

Currency moves are difficult to predict, but looking at the recent move in the U.S. Dollar, it does appear to have reached some resistance from a technical perspective, i.e., making lower highs as noted by the downward sloping red dotted line on the below chart. The green dotted line represents support and if the Dollar breaks below that, further weakening could take place; thus, serving as a tailwind for international returns.

In reviewing weekly mutual fund and ETF flows, investors are not embracing the potential investment opportunity in international markets. Over the course of the last twelve months, the cumulative flow to global equities is a negative -$27.3 billion, while U.S. equity inflows have totaled $218.8 billion.

Certainly, equity returns outside of the U.S. have been mostly challenging over the last number of years, as noted earlier. The below year-to-date return chart is a short one, not yet two months, however, the three international indexes shown on the chart are all outperforming the S&P 500 Index. One area of the U.S. market that has been a tailwind for investors is the mega cap Magnificent 7 stocks. A lot has been written about the market over the last two years and the fact that returns have been driven by a narrow group of stocks., i.e., strength in the Mag 7 stocks, but year-to-date, the MAGS ETF is up 2.1% and trails the S&P 500 Index's year-to-date return. For investors, there might be an opportunity for some international exposure in one's portfolio, keeping in mind multinational U.S. companies have exposure in the international markets too. Also, higher interest rates tend to translate into a stronger Dollar, all else being equal, and recent commentary by the Fed suggests they will be more hawkish on rates.

The international environment faces some headwinds, not the least of which is the continued war in Ukraine and tariff issues proposed by the U.S. If some of these issues are resolved favorably, though, international equities might find some support or better returns ahead. Since the international versus U.S. outperformance tends to run in a long multi-year cycle, an investor being late versus early to increase international exposure certainly would be reasonable. Nonetheless, the international asset class is one investors might want to keep on their radar.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Twilio: A Warranted Pullback (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Last Friday, one of the market's biggest losers was Twilio (NYSE:TWLO), after the company reported its fiscal fourth quarter results. The customer engagement platform had been one of the market's biggest winners over the past year, but I recently warned investors that near term expectations were a bit inflated. With the latest results in and the stock having pulled back a bit from its recent multi-year high, the situation looks quite a bit better now.

Previous coverage of the name:

I last covered the name in late January, right after the stock had surged to a new high after the company's Investor Day, at which point I urged a bit of caution. With management pre-announcing Q4 revenues that were better than expected, while laying out the plan for the next couple of years, I thought that near term upside would be limited. Analyst estimates had already risen to the top end the company's 2025 revenue guidance range, meaning the street was expecting a guidance raise at some point this year.

With the stock's valuation now pretty much in the middle of my fair value range, I figured there was more near term room for disappointment than another upside surprise. That happened to be the case, with shares having fallen 14% since my previous article, while the S&P 500 has had a slight gain. After peaking at nearly $152 recently, Twilio shares closed last Friday at just over $125, their lowest close since the post-Investor Day pop.

The Q4 earnings report:

For the quarter, Twilio announced revenues of $1.19 billion. The more than 11% growth was the best figure since Q2 2023, with solid acceleration from the less than 4.5% seen just two quarters earlier. The company did top street estimates by almost $13 million, although this was the smallest beat in nearly two years thanks to the pre-announcement that called for 11% growth.

The good news here is that some of the key metrics I've focused on in the past are showing solid improvement. The company increased its active customer account base by over 20,000 in the past year, the best result of the past five quarters. Deferred revenue and customer deposits also hit a new high, and their sequential growth rate was the best in three years. As the chart below shows, the dollar-based net expansion rate ticked up another percentage point to 106%. This was the highest level since Q1 2023 indicating the potential for further revenue growth in the coming quarters.

Q4 non-GAAP operating income was a record at $197 million, up 14% year-over-year, thanks to strong revenue growth and cost discipline. The company did report its first-ever GAAP operating profit quarter, although it did record a $17 million bad debt expense in the period due to a problem with a customer in Brazil that had been previously disclosed. Overall, adjusted earnings per share missed by 3 cents, which was quite different than the usual large bottom line beats investors were used to.

Looking to the first-quarter of 2025, Twilio expects to earn between $0.88 and $0.93 per share on an adjusted basis, with revenues forecast to be between $1.13 billion and $1.14 billion. Analysts had gone into last week's report expecting adjusted earnings of $0.98 per share and $1.14 billion in revenue. I should remind everyone that this management has been very conservative with guidance over the years, and it is not a surprise to see a light forecast given and then decent beats come in a couple of months.

I had mentioned in my previous article that management had already laid out guidance for the next couple of years at the Investor Day. This included 2025 revenue guidance for growth of 7% to 8%, and analysts were expecting the top end of that range. Thus, when Twilio only maintained that guidance last week, it was seen as a slight disappointment, especially given the stock's massive rise recently. The street is now expecting that 8% top line growth this year, meaning analysts think a guidance raise will come at some point. Non-GAAP operating income and free cash flow guidance ranges for this year were also reiterated.

The company's balance sheet remains healthy with over $2.3 billion in cash and investments against just $1 billion in total debt. Management expects about $3 billion in cumulative free cash flow through 2027, while expecting to return an average of 50% to investors annually. I stated previously that we'd likely see a near term increase to the share count as a result of a slower buyback, and management did guide to a sequential increase for non-GAAP weighted average diluted shares outstanding in Q1 2025.

A look at current valuation:

I mentioned previously that if the revenue story continued to improve in the coming quarters, I thought that a mid-single digit price to sales ratio, or about 4 to 6 times expected yearly revenues, would be fairly reasonable, given the much improved bottom-line picture. Management has essentially guided to this growth already for the next few years, potentially taking out room for upside surprises. With the price to sales ratio topping 4.70 times in late January, I was a bit more neutral then. However, the recent pullback combined with slightly higher estimates now has that number back down to around 4.00 times, a much more favorable place.

I also mentioned how analyst price targets were seemingly playing catch up to the sharp rally in recent months. About three weeks ago, analysts were expecting about 14% downside in the stock. However, the average street target has risen more than $13 since my latest article, with the new $141 plus figure now representing about 10% upside from last week's finishing price.

Final thoughts and recommendation:

While Twilio had a mostly decent report last week, the company had already pre-announced a number of results and there was a huge stock rally over the past year. Thus, the Q4 report became mostly one of those "buy the rumor, sell the news" types of events. I previously warned investors to be careful in late January, with the potential for limited upside surprises in the near term.

A few weeks later, the scenario looks quite a bit different, which is why I am upgrading shares back to a buy rating today. The company's key metrics in Q4 all showed solid improvement, and management still is expecting decent revenue, adjusted operating income, and free cash flow growth over the next couple of years. The valuation picture has also improved rather nicely, with the average street price target representing double-digit upside instead of nearly mid-teens downside. I don't expect shares to rally back to their recent high right away, but I could see the stock moving higher over time, especially if management does end up raising guidance throughout 2025.

data.title: Why Inflation And 'Real Interest' Rates Are Bullish For Gold
data.description:
By Mike Maharrey

It’s clear that the Federal Reserve hasn’t won the inflation fight. With the CPI trending higher for four straight months, the markets aren’t pricing in another interest rate cut until September.

Isn’t a “higher for longer” interest rate environment bearish for gold? The short answer is no because real interest rates are falling with price inflation on the upswing.

Why Do Higher Interest Rates Create Headwinds for Gold

Gold is a “non-yielding” asset, meaning it does not generate interest income or dividends. Its value is determined by price appreciation. Conventional wisdom is that investors turn to bonds and other yielding assets in a higher interest rate environment, creating headwinds for gold.

We saw this phenomenon play out when the Fed was hiking rates to battle price inflation a couple of years ago. Every time we got bad inflation data, gold sold off with the expectation of more rate increases.

But when the January CPI data came in higher than expected last week, gold’s price dip was short-lived, and it quickly recovered to $2,900. It appears that at least some investors have figured out that inflation isn’t going anywhere, and they need gold as an inflation hedge despite the higher interest rate environment.

Real Interest Rates Are Falling

There is another factor in play. With price inflation increasing, real interest rates are falling even as the Federal Reserve puts rate cuts on hold. The real interest rate is simply the stated rate you see on the news adjusted for price inflation.

To calculate the real interest rate, you take the quoted interest rate and subtract CPI.

The Federal funds rate is currently set at 4.5 percent. When you factor in the CPI of 3 percent, the real rate is only 1.5 percent. (4.5-3=1.5). If the CPI moves to 3.5 percent and the Fed holds rates steady, the real interest rate will fall to 1.0 percent.

Note that in a low interest rate environment or if price inflation is particularly high, real interest rates can go negative. For instance, if the Fed cut rates to 2 percent while the CPI remained at 3 percent, the real interest rate would be -1.0 percent.

Keep in mind that the CPI doesn’t tell the entire story of inflation. The government revised the CPI formula in the 1990s so that it understated the actual rise in prices. Based on the formula used in the 1970s, CPI is closer to double the official numbers. So, if the BLS was using the old formula, we’re looking at CPI closer to 6 percent. And using an honest formula, it would probably be worse than that.

This means that as price inflation creeps up, the opportunity cost of holding gold is dropping even if the Fed holds interest rates steady. (Opportunity cost refers to the interest you could have earned if you had bought a bond instead of a bar of gold.)

Will the Fed Hike Rates?

The Federal Reserve could counter falling real rates by pushing the federal funds rate higher. But so far, the central bank has given no indication that rate hikes are on the table. In fact, the consensus is that the Fed has merely put cuts on hold for the time being and will likely resume monetary easing in the fall.

Given the trajectory of price inflation and the fact that the central bank never did enough to slay the inflation dragon, the central bank probably should consider pushing rates higher to get price inflation under control. But the Fed is in a Catch-22.

Given the level of debt in the economy, coupled with all the malinvestments caused by well over a decade of artificially low interest rates, the economy can’t function even in a moderately tight interest rate environment. Simply put, the economy is addicted to easy money.

This is precisely why everybody is desperate for rate cuts, and the Fed delivered in December while simultaneously jawboning about caution and trying to dampen expectations of more rate cuts in 2025.

On the other hand, it’s clear that inflation remains stubbornly sticky. Here’s how Reuters summed up the dilemma:

“Extreme bond market agitation has put the Federal Reserve in a bind. It can either cool long-term inflation fears or acquiesce to President-elect Donald Trump's complaints about interest rates being "far too high." It can't do both and will likely opt to tackle the former, potentially setting up a running verbal battle with the White House over the coming year.” [Emphasis added]

The Reuters report goes on to say, “The Fed has routinely stated that containing inflation expectations is one of its primary roles,” and it’s “hard to imagine” the central bank will ignore signs of revived price inflation.

They can say that now, but it becomes easier to “imagine” when the economy starts to crack under the weight of high interest rates. Simply put, the Fed needs to simultaneously cut rates and keep rates higher. Good luck with that.

The bottom line is that even with price inflation warming up and rate cuts on hold, it is highly unlikely that the central bank will start raising rates.

If they do start talking more rate hikes, this would likely cause headwinds for gold. But given the current environment, inflation is in control, and real rates are falling.

This is bullish for gold and likely contributing to the push toward $3,000 per ounce.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JELD-WEN Holding, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by JELD-WEN Holding, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: AvePoint: Trending Well, But Q4 Profitability May Not Be As Strong As Q3
data.description:
Introduction

AvePoint, Inc. (NASDAQ:AVPT), a mid-cap SaaS proxy, noted for its expertise in data management and data governance, has served as an outstanding source of alpha for its stakeholders over the past year. In a year where its peers from the Russell 2000 have generated decent gains of +13%, and other software & services stocks have fared even better (with a return differential of 2x the Russell 2000), AVPT has flourished in a different orbit altogether, with returns of over 140%!

If you scrutinize the image above, you’ll note that the AVPT stock had been faring reasonably well till around November, after which we saw an explosive thrust. Much of this was on account of a sturdy Q3 earnings event towards the end of the first week of November 2024. So, can AVPT repeat the trick yet again, and kickstart another leg higher after it publishes its Q4 earnings on the 27th of February (post market hours)?

Well, it’s difficult to say, but here are a few pointers that could provide some useful context on what to expect.

Upcoming Earnings - Key Metrics To Watch

If AVPT is to fare as well as it did after the Q3 event, we’d need to first contextualize the headlines from that event and see if AVPT is well positioned to hit those heights yet again.

Note that in the preceding 5 quarters leading up to Q3-24, AVPT had been delivering an average topline beat of 4% (relative to consensus), with revenue coming in at the $72-$78m mark for 4 straight quarters. In Q3, things finally went off script, with a much superior beat of 6.5% and sales almost approaching the $90 mark.

Seasonal factors certainly played a part in Q3 (something which will likely abet AVPT yet again in Q4, as H2 has traditionally been stronger for the company), but it’s worth noting that expectations have already been lifted for Q4; to highlight, over the past 3 months, the Q4 topline has already been raised by roughly 3%.

Having said that, note that the expected revenue figure of $88.06m, is also a tad lower than what was seen in Q3 ($88.8m). Besides for growth stocks like AVPT whose valuations are founded on consistently impressive business growth rates every quarter (it has been averaging YoY topline growth of 22% for the last 3 quarters) it may also be unpleasant to note that the topline growth in Q4 is poised to only come in at 18% (also lower than its 3-year topline growth of 20%). All in all, we suspect AVPT may have to deliver a Q4 topline over $95m mark, to truly whet the appetite of the bulls.

Progress on the absolute revenue front, won’t be the only key revenue metric worth watching; in fact quite a few discerning stakeholders of AVPT would rather turn their attention to the pace of growth in the annual recurring revenue (ARR) profile (recently crossed the $300m landmark for the first time), which had declined to the 23% levels 5 quarters ago, and stayed rather consistent since then.

Speaking of AVPT’s ARR, one of the more comforting facets is that it is not inordinately exposed towards the tailwinds and headwinds of any particular sector, and hedges its bets across a dozen sectors. However, note that Q3 has typically benefitted from heightened momentum in the public sector vertical (which is one of AVPT’s more important verticals), and that uplift may not necessarily persist in Q4.

Another key monitorable for the investment community will be AVPT’s dollar-based gross retention rate, which is essentially a yardstick on how satisfied AVPT’s active customers are with its services (does not include the ARR generated from new customers). After hovering at the 87% levels for 3 straight quarters, this had inched up to 88%, and is now only around 200bps away from their long-term target of 90%. If AVPT can continue to demonstrate further progress on this front, it will be taken well.

While the existing active customers are spending a healthy amount across AVPT’s platform, a lot of curiosity will also go towards the pace of net new ARR growth, which has been exceeding total ARR growth, and growing at its highest pace (31% YoY) since the company went public in mid-2021.

Most people would attribute success here to the effectiveness of AVPT’s direct sales force, but an underappreciated facet of the recent growth is likely also driven by AVPT’s indirect channels (their partner ecosystem and managed services providers which can be very effective for garnering SMB clients). Investors should note that historically AVPT has largely catered to large enterprises in highly regulated industries, but off late they’ve increasingly been focusing efforts towards acquiring SMB clients (businesses with sub 500 employees) by leveraging the managed service provider (MSP) expertise.

On the cost and margin front, even though AVPT’s sales force is becoming more productive, Q4 could suffer as around $1m of OPEX spend that was budgeted for Q3 gets pushed into the final quarter of the year. In addition to that, management had also highlighted, their desire to step up their programmatic marketing investments in Q4; as a result, both these metrics, which had been trending lower recently, could once again spike in Q4.

Needless to say, this won’t reflect well on the non-GAAP operating margin which had hit impressive levels of 20.1% in Q3. Expect a step-down closer to the 15% levels in Q4. For context, AVPT’s long-term target is to hit levels of 20-25% on the non-GAAP operating margin front.

Having said that, despite some hiccups on the cost front in Q4, we don’t envisage AVPT not hitting GAAP profitability for the full year (it is already GAAP profitable for the first 9 months of the year)

Going by historical trends, cash flow generation could also be particularly resilient in Q4 (over the last couple of years, it has emerged as the strongest cash flow generating quarter) and could help the FCF yield which has dipped in recent months (mainly on account of the surge in the market-cap) get closer to levels of 3%.

That should continue to boost AVPT’s cash position, which already accounts for a mammoth proportion of the total asset base (53% of the asset base).

Normally, when a company maintains such as huge cash war chest on its balance sheet, we often see this being deployed by way of a higher cadence of share repurchases, but in AVPT’s case, momentum has ironically been declining (perhaps due to a surge in the share price and valuations). Interestingly enough, it’s worth noting that AVPT’s $150m share buyback plan, announced in March 2022, is due to expire next month so one does wonder if there will be a step-up in share buyback activity in Q4.

Closing Thoughts - Is AVPT Stock A Good Buy Now?

AvePoint’s long-term prospects look particularly bright in an era where the usage of unstructured data and generative-AI is on the up. Currently, a lot of potential clients in the marketplace are not able to get their AI use cases to fruition because they don’t have a robust data management and data governance framework. AvePoint is well-positioned to help ameliorate these challenges as it has been developing cloud-native expertise in this field for over two decades now and currently offers automated solutions across multi-cloud environments.

In light of these promising circumstances, the stock is currently witnessing tremendous momentum, trading above its 3 key moving averages (50, 100 and 200DMA) on the daily chart.

Having said that, if you take a look at the monthly chart, you’d note that the stock is now at record highs (which is never a great time for value investors to kickstart a long position) and is also trading above its upper Bollinger band (which represents 2 standard deviations away from the 20-month moving average).

The stock’s valuations too are worth noting, particularly in light of the earnings growth on offer. Over the next couple of years, AVPT will likely deliver earnings growth of less than 37% p.a., yet it is priced at a very steep P/E of 83x, implying a PEG ratio of almost 2.25x which is not ideal.

Note that other software and services stocks (as represented by the XSW ETF) are priced at a significant P/E discount of 69% (26x P/E).

It also doesn't help that AVPT's relative strength ratio versus other software and services offerings, which has averaged around 0.064 across is trading history, is now priced at a premium of 56%, dampening the incentive to go long.

Considering all these factors, we wouldn't rate the stock as a buy, but rather a hold.

data.title: Gold Stocks Vs. Bullion: Why The Lag?
data.description:
You might think that the price of physical gold and the performance of gold mining stocks should more or less always move together, since higher gold prices typically boost miners’ revenues. However, there are instances — like now — where physical gold prices surge while gold mining stocks stay flat or even decline. There are a lot of reasons this can happen, but it sometimes represents a buying opportunity.

Take 2000-2011, which of course includes the 2008 financial crisis. The price of physical gold rocketed up over 500%, but gold mining equities returned almost 700% during the same decade. During bull markets, the cost of gold production rises slower than the value per ounce of bullion, which gold miners can leverage.

However, any number of market factors could cause a gold mining stock to underperform bullion in the shorter term despite a boom in bullion prices, but the lag usually doesn’t last. This isn’t to say that gold mining stocks are a replacement for physical gold in a portfolio. Unlike physical gold, they aren’t money — they’re stocks. But they’re inextricably linked to gold, and as part of a diversified precious metals' investment portfolio, Peter Schiff still recommends allocating investment capital to gold miners, especially when they appear undervalued.

Kinross Gold (KGC) was down about 7% last week simply because analysts overshot the mark and expected earnings that Kinross didn’t meet. That’s in spite of an objectively strong 82% rise in earnings. But oftentimes, when markets react, they overreact.

Other times, inflationary pressure, rising prices, labor shortages, trouble at gold mines where a company is invested, legal surprises, and other factors can temporarily drag down mining stocks even as gold booms. In 2024, Barrick Gold Corporation (GOLD) had to shut down a mine in Mali due to a dispute with authorities relating to new mining laws. Barrick hopes to reopen the mine, but had to revise their 2025 output projections because of the closure.

These kinds of geopolitical risks can disrupt production and negatively impact investor confidence, causing stock prices to fall even when gold prices are rising. Unlike Kinross, however, Barrick’s recent earnings and production report caused the stock to jump. But it’s still currently undervalued relative to the boom in bullion, which is likely to continue in 2025.

Barrick Gold Corp 5-Day Chart

Gold stocks can also go down due to investors reacting to gold and silver spreads. With the gold/silver ratio currently around $90, investors may be unwinding their positions to take profits or cut losses. As Peter Schiff noted last Friday on X/Twitter:

“Yes, gold is down nearly $40 today, but there’s no reason for gold mining stocks to be down too. Gold is barely below $2,900, and prices are going much higher. Yet most gold stocks would be cheap at twice the price. Some of today’s decline may be unwinding of gold/silver spreads.”

Another problem with the short-term valuation of gold stocks is coming from the inability of Wall Street analysts to understand and properly model when it comes to gold and gold miners. Forecasting earnings for these mining companies, in part, means having to forecast the price of bullion itself.

The Fed is not in control. Inflation is ripping as the money supply expands, prices are rising, and gold will respond with further gains. But Wall Street remains clueless about gold, which affects the valuation of mining stocks. As Peter Schiff said recently on X Spaces:

“All the Wall Street firms, when they rate these gold stocks, they assume the price of gold is going to be much lower in the future than it is now. They have no confidence in this rally. That’s why they don’t want to buy gold stocks because they expect the price of gold to fall, even though it’s going to continue to rise.”

Regardless of when Wall Street and retail investors figure it out, the yellow metal remains the undisputed safe-haven asset, especially with inflation hot and the possibility of QE later this year to “save” the system. Hard money will never change. But the broader global precious metals market is more than just physical.

With gold mining stocks potentially outperforming bullion during certain time frames, and a pattern of being undervalued during this latest bull market, diversifying into a group of winning miners is a move worth considering as gold readies to surpass $3,000 for the first time in history.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rezolute: Late-Stage Study Targeting Hyperinsulinism
data.description:
Rezolute (NASDAQ:RZLT) is a Redwood City, Ca based microcap biopharma developing therapies for hypoglycemia caused by hyperinsulinism. Despite its small size, it is in late-stage trials, with trial data available from earlier stages. This makes it interesting to look into. The company was founded in 2010 and was earlier known as AntriaBio. It changed its name to Rezolute in 2017; however, the company has retained its insulin focus throughout.

RZLT’s lead asset is RZ358 (ersodetug), an IV administered mAb that binds to an insulin receptor at a unique, allosteric site in target tissues like the liver and elsewhere. In this way, it aims to control insulin upregulation and normalizes glucose. It has a half-life of over two weeks and can be potentially administered only once a month for hyperinsulin patients.

The phase 3 trial is in Congenital Hyperinsulinism (cHI), and they are expecting topline data in 2H 2025. A second program is enrolling patients in a phase 3 study in Tumor Hyperinsulinism (tHI), and they are expecting to complete enrollment by 1H 2025. Their second asset, RZ402, has proof of concept data in Diabetic Macular Edema (DME), and is looking for partners.

Target indications

There are three - Congenital Hyperinsulinism, Tumor Hyperinsulinism, and Diabetic Macular Edema. cHI is a rare genetic disease where pancreatic ß-cells secrete too much insulin in children, which causes hypoglycemia, or too little sugar, and can lead to seizures, developmental delays, even death. There are two main forms, diffuse, which involves the whole pancreas, and focal, which involves parts of it.

In 2021, the incidence of HI in the US was estimated to be 1 in 30,000 to 50,000 new births, which means there are between 2,000 and 4,000 Americans with congenital HI. The global market size is small (according to this research), at around a projected size of $377mn in 2031. However, if there are 3000 patients in the US, and an equal number in the EU, I don’t think these estimates are correct. If treatment costs $50,000 per year per patient, then we have $300mn for these two markets alone, in 2021 figures.

Diazoxide has been used to treat HI for decades, with varying results. In patients unresponsive to diazoxide, off-label use of octreotide, a somatostatin analogue, has produced results. Severe cases may require surgical intervention, either partial or total pancreatectomy. There is a severe unmet need in this indication. Among pipeline drugs, ersodetug takes the lead. Next is Hanmi Pharma’s Efpegerglucagon, which is in phase 2a trials. Current therapies mainly work by inhibiting insulin secretion, while ersodetug aims to regulate insulin receptor signaling, offering a potentially universal therapeutic approach for various forms of HI.

Moving on, Tumor Hyperinsulinism is caused by insulinomas, a rare type of neuroendocrine tumor occurring in the pancreas. These secrete insulin independently of what the blood glucose level is, causing hypoglycemia. Patients present with the same symptoms of any other kind of HI. There are only about 4 cases of TH per million people in the US. So, it is an ultra-rare disease with just about 1200 American patients.

Surgical resection is the standard of care and is successful. Other forms of tumor debulking include chemo and radiation. In patients that are not amenable to, or refractory to, surgery, diazoxide and other nonspecific drugs may be used. There are no pipeline drugs that I am aware of.

Data

The phase 2b RIZE study produced data in 2022. The study looked at young kids, with the average age being around 6 and a half. It included a mix of boys and girls from different genetic backgrounds. To join the study, kids needed to have serious low blood sugar issues, even if they were already on standard treatments. At the start, they were spending about 25% of their time with low blood sugar.

The drug, RZ358, was generally safe and didn’t cause major side effects. The right therapeutic amount of the drug was found in the body after dosing, and it worked better at higher doses. Overall, RZ358 cut down low blood sugar by about 50% across all groups. The best results were seen with the 6 mg/kg and 9 mg/kg doses, which improved low blood sugar by around 75%.

In tumor HI as well, the company reported data from a patient who was treated with RZ538. Data showed that in this one patient with severe hypoglycemia caused by malignant insulinoma, Hypoglycemia was completely resolved after treatment with RZ358. Details:

Following a 6 mg/kg dose of RZ358, there was transient worsening of hypoglycemia accompanied by an 8-fold increase in insulin, potentially due to reduced insulin clearance. After dose increase to 9 mg/kg weekly, glucose infusion was weaned. Metabolic stability was achieved after 6 doses, allowing a second dose of Lu177 and eventual discharge. Diazoxide was discontinued, steroid doses were reduced, and RZ358 dosing was reduced to every 3-4 weeks. A third dose of Lu-177 was administered without complications. Despite elevated insulin (537 uIU/mL), C-peptide (10.1 ng/mL), and proinsulin (634.0 pmol/L), he remains free of level 3 hypoglycemia. No adverse effects have been observed.

Coming to DME, the company has data from last year for RZ402, an oral Plasma Kallikrein inhibitor. The study met the primary endpoint of clinically significant reduction in Central subfield thickness (CST). The company states that RZ402 is the “first PKI and oral therapy of any class to demonstrate a CST improvement in patients with DME.” However, since the company does not plan to move ahead with the asset without a partner, we will not focus more time on this.

Financials

RZLT has a market cap of $279mn and a cash balance of $105mn. Research and development expenses were $12.6 million for the second quarter of fiscal 2025, while general and administrative expenses were $4.5 million. Thus, they have a cash runway of 5–6 quarters.

Looking at FCF, their CFO was ($13.7mn) while there was no CapEx. Thus, their CFO was ($13.7mn) and cash runway comes to 6–7 quarters.

Risks

RZLT is quite derisked because there is very little competition, and they are in phase 3 stage with good phase 2 data. The only issue is the small market size and the cheap availability of diazoxide. These two factors together make RZLT somewhat risky, not in terms of approval, but in terms of market penetration.

Bottom line

RZLT is doing interesting science and deserves investor interest. I have said that the market size is small, but that statement needs to be qualified by the fact that since there are no major approved therapies, especially for patients for whom SoC does not work well, it is difficult to put an unambiguous estimate for the market. Thus, one may like to take a small pilot position in this stock based on the upcoming phase 3 data catalyst.

data.title: Hedge Funds Buying Single-Family Homes: The Impact On Housing Affordability And Market Dynamics
data.description:
When it comes to housing, hedge funds buying single-family homes has become a hot topic. These big players in the financial world are diving into the real estate market, snapping up properties that could have gone to individual buyers. Critics say this trend is pushing home prices up and making it harder for regular folks to own a home. Others argue the impact isn’t as big as it seems. Either way, it’s clear that hedge funds are changing the game in the housing market.

Understanding Hedge Funds and Their Role in the Housing Market

What Are Hedge Funds and Institutional Investors?

Hedge funds are investment vehicles that pool money from wealthy individuals or institutions to generate high returns. Unlike traditional investment funds, hedge funds often use complex strategies like short selling, leverage, and derivatives. Institutional investors, such as pension funds, endowments, and sovereign wealth funds, are entities that invest large sums of money on behalf of others. Both hedge funds and institutional investors are increasingly active in real estate, including single-family homes.

How Hedge Funds Differ From Traditional Homebuyers

Hedge funds and institutional investors operate very differently from individual homebuyers:

  • Scale of Purchases: Hedge funds often buy dozens or even hundreds of homes at a time.
  • Payment Method: They typically pay in cash, which makes their offers more attractive to sellers.
  • Purpose: Unlike families looking for a place to live, hedge funds aim to generate rental income or flip properties for profit.

This approach can make it hard for regular buyers to compete, as they often rely on mortgages and cannot match the speed or terms offered by institutional investors.

The Rise of Hedge Funds in Single-Family Home Investments

In recent years, hedge funds have increased their focus on single-family homes. This trend is fueled by several factors:

  1. Stable Returns: Single-family rentals provide consistent income, even during economic downturns.
  2. Data and Technology: Advanced algorithms help identify profitable properties quickly.
  3. Market Conditions: Low interest rates and a housing shortage have made real estate an attractive investment.

The growing presence of hedge funds in the housing market has sparked debates about their impact on affordability and community dynamics. While they bring efficiency and capital, their large-scale operations can also disrupt traditional market forces.

The Impact of Hedge Funds on Housing Affordability

How Hedge Fund Purchases Influence Home Prices

Hedge funds and institutional investors have been buying up single-family homes, often outbidding traditional homebuyers. This trend has contributed to rising home prices, making it harder for average families to afford homes. By targeting specific markets, hedge funds create localized scarcity, which drives prices even higher. For instance, in some cities, investors account for nearly 20% of all home sales, amplifying competition and pricing out individual buyers.

The Effect on Rental Markets and Tenants

When hedge funds acquire homes, they often convert them into rental properties. This shift increases demand in the rental market, pushing rents higher. Tenants may face:

  • Limited housing options due to reduced availability of owner-occupied homes.
  • Higher rental costs, as institutional landlords aim to maximize profits.
  • Less flexibility, with stricter lease terms and less responsive property management.

Communities Most Affected by Hedge Fund Activity

Not all areas are equally impacted. Hedge funds tend to focus on:

  1. Urban centers and growing suburbs with high demand.
  2. Communities of color, where homeownership rates are already lower.
  3. Regions with historically affordable housing, turning them into high-rent districts.

The concentration of investor activity in certain neighborhoods has reshaped local markets, often displacing long-term residents and altering the community fabric.

Market Dynamics Shaped by Hedge Fund Investments

Geographic Trends in Hedge Fund Home Purchases

Hedge funds tend to focus their investments in specific regions, often targeting metropolitan areas with growing populations and strong rental demand. Cities like Charlotte, Atlanta, and Phoenix have seen significant activity, with some neighborhoods experiencing hedge funds purchasing large percentages of available homes. This concentration can lead to localized market distortions, where home prices and rents rise faster than in surrounding areas.

The Role of iBuyers and Algorithmic Buying

iBuyers, such as Opendoor and Offerpad, use algorithms to make instant cash offers on homes, aiming for quick transactions. Hedge funds often partner with or emulate these companies to streamline their acquisitions. This technology-driven approach allows them to outcompete traditional buyers, especially in competitive markets. However, it also raises concerns about the fairness and transparency of these practices, as algorithmic buying can inflate local housing prices.

Shifts in Market Share Among Institutional Investors

While hedge funds have gained attention, they represent only a fraction of the overall housing market. Smaller "mom-and-pop" investors still dominate the single-family rental sector. However, institutional investors are growing their market share, with projections suggesting they could control up to 40% of single-family rental homes by 2030. This trend could reshape the housing landscape, potentially reducing opportunities for individual homebuyers.

Institutional investors are not just buying homes; they are changing how the housing market operates. Their financial resources and strategic approaches are setting new norms in housing transactions, for better or worse.

Challenges Faced by Hedge Funds in Single-Family Home Investments

Operational Inefficiencies in Scaling Investments

Hedge funds face significant challenges when trying to scale investments in single-family homes. Unlike large apartment complexes or commercial buildings, single-family homes must often be purchased one at a time. This creates inefficiencies in both time and cost. The process of identifying, acquiring, and managing individual properties is far more labor-intensive than other types of real estate investments.

Some key hurdles include:

  • Hyperlocal Analysis: Each property’s value can vary dramatically, even within the same neighborhood. Hedge funds need extensive data and resources to evaluate properties on a block-by-block basis.
  • Property Management: Managing hundreds or thousands of single-family homes can be a logistical nightmare. Funds must decide whether to outsource management or build in-house teams — both of which have drawbacks.
  • Technology Gaps: While algorithms and software have improved processes, they haven’t eliminated the complexities of scaling single-family home investments.

The Influence of Rising Interest Rates

Rising interest rates are another obstacle for hedge funds. Although institutional investors don’t rely on traditional mortgages, they often use other forms of leverage to finance their purchases. As interest rates climb, the cost of borrowing increases, squeezing profit margins. This has led many hedge funds to pull back from the market in recent years.

Higher borrowing costs limit the ability of hedge funds to achieve the returns they target, making single-family homes less attractive compared to other investment options.

Why Hedge Funds Are Selling Off Properties

In recent years, some hedge funds have begun selling off portions of their single-family home portfolios. This trend is driven by several factors:

  1. Market Saturation: In some areas, the competition for properties has driven up prices, reducing the potential for future appreciation.
  2. Operational Challenges: The issues with scaling and managing large portfolios have made single-family homes less appealing.
  3. Strategic Shifts: Some funds are reallocating their capital into other asset classes, such as multifamily developments or commercial real estate, which offer better scalability.

These challenges highlight the complexities of institutional investments in single-family homes, underscoring why this market segment isn’t as straightforward as it might seem at first glance.

Debunking Myths About Hedge Funds and Housing

The Scale of Hedge Fund Ownership in the Housing Market

The belief that hedge funds are buying up all the homes and driving regular buyers out of the market has gained traction, but the numbers tell a different story. Institutional investors, including hedge funds, accounted for just 14.8% of home purchases in early 2024. While this is significant, it’s far from the majority. Even at its peak in late 2022, investor-owned homes made up 28.7% of home sales — meaning the vast majority of homes are still being purchased by individuals and families. The narrative that hedge funds dominate the housing market is exaggerated and doesn’t reflect the broader reality.

Here’s a quick breakdown:

  • Most homes are still owned by individual buyers, not institutions.
  • Institutional investors often focus on specific markets, like large cities or high-demand neighborhoods.
  • By 2030, institutional investors are projected to own 40% of single-family rental homes, not the entire housing market.

Misleading Statistics and Public Perception

Statistics around hedge fund activity in housing can be misleading without proper context. For example, while some neighborhoods have seen institutional investors buying up to 50% of homes, this is not the national norm. These numbers are often cherry-picked to create a narrative of widespread market takeover. Additionally, hedge funds are an easy target for public frustration about housing affordability. It’s simpler to blame a faceless entity than to address systemic issues like zoning laws, construction costs, and supply shortages.

Public perception often oversimplifies the issue, ignoring the fact that housing affordability is a complex problem tied to multiple factors beyond hedge fund activity.

Legislative Proposals and Their Potential Impact

In response to public outcry, legislators have proposed various bills to limit institutional investors’ activity in the housing market. These proposals range from increased taxes on bulk home purchases to outright bans on hedge funds owning residential properties. While these measures may curb some investor activity, they’re unlikely to solve the broader affordability crisis. The real problem lies in insufficient housing supply, not just who is buying the homes.

Potential legislative impacts include:

  1. Reducing institutional investor activity in high-demand areas.
  2. Encouraging more individual buyers to enter the market.
  3. Potentially increasing rents if investors shift focus to rental properties.

In conclusion, hedge funds play a role in the housing market, but they are not the sole — or even the primary — cause of housing affordability challenges. Addressing the root issues requires a broader approach, including building more homes and revisiting zoning policies.

The Future of Hedge Funds in the Housing Market

Predictions for Hedge Fund Activity in Real Estate

Looking ahead, hedge funds are expected to remain active participants in the housing market, but their strategies may shift. Rising interest rates and operational inefficiencies have already pushed some funds to reevaluate their portfolios. While some firms may scale back, others might pivot to focus on markets with higher returns or diversify into mixed-use developments. Expect to see hedge funds becoming more selective in their acquisitions, targeting properties with strong rental income potential or long-term appreciation prospects.

Emerging Trends in Institutional Investments

Institutional investors are increasingly leveraging technology to optimize their real estate strategies. Algorithmic buying and data-driven decision-making are likely to play a bigger role, enabling funds to identify undervalued properties more efficiently. Additionally, there’s growing interest in sustainable housing projects, as investors respond to both regulatory pressures and market demand for eco-friendly homes.

  • Increased adoption of blockchain for property transactions to improve transparency and efficiency.
  • Expansion into affordable housing initiatives, driven by public pressure and potential tax incentives.
  • Partnerships with local developers to mitigate community pushback and streamline operations.

Potential Solutions to Address Housing Affordability

The role of hedge funds in housing affordability has sparked significant debate. Some proposed solutions include:

  1. Legislative Caps: Limiting the number of single-family homes that institutional investors can own in a given market.
  2. Incentives for Affordable Housing: Offering tax breaks or subsidies to funds investing in affordable rental units.
  3. Community Engagement: Encouraging hedge funds to collaborate with local governments and nonprofits to address housing shortages.

"Balancing profitability with social responsibility will be a key challenge for hedge funds in the housing market moving forward."

Conclusion

The role of hedge funds and institutional investors in the single-family housing market is a complex and often misunderstood topic. While their presence has grown in recent years, their overall impact on housing affordability and market dynamics remains a subject of debate. Data suggests that institutional investors account for a relatively small percentage of home purchases and ownership, with "mom-and-pop" landlords continuing to dominate the rental market. However, the perception of these large entities as market disruptors persists, fueled by political narratives and selective statistics. As policymakers and communities grapple with housing challenges, it is essential to consider all contributing factors, including supply shortages and local zoning laws, rather than focusing solely on institutional investment. A balanced understanding of these dynamics is crucial for addressing the broader issues of housing affordability and accessibility.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
